full_text:
Spatial analysis of homologous recombination-deficient tumors identifies myeloid-mediated topological networks associated with immune exhaustion

Introduction: Homologous recombination (HR) is a fundamental DNA repair pathway in charge of maintaining genome stability. HR deficiency (HRD) is a driver of tumorigenesis and often indicative of sensitivity to targeted therapy with PARP inhibitors, making its detection, germline or somatic, of high clinical importance. Nonetheless, treatment resistance has been recognized as a major challenge in the clinic and little is known about the immunogenetic landscape underlying the tumor microenvironment (TME) and its effect on tumor progression.

Objective: Identify spatial patterns driving immune evasion and tumor development in the TME of HRD tumors using spatial omics.

Materials and methods: We performed multiplexed immunofluorescence imaging (t-CycIF) in 33 samples from squamous cell carcinomas from patients with and without pathognomonic germline HRD syndrome, Fanconi anemia (FA) and non-FA, respectively. H&E of adjacent tissue cuts from each sample were used to map histopathological annotations in the images acquired (LGD: low grade dysplasia, HGD: high grade dysplasia, CIS: carcinoma in-situ, IC: invasive carcinoma). Cells were segmented (StarDist) and each marker quantified (MCMICRO) as the median marker intensity within each cell. Finally, single-cell expression matrixes were analyzed using state-of-the-art computational methods to identify spatial features related to immune suppression and cancer progression. Cellular neighborhood identification (SCIMAP) was used to identify repeating cellular niches, while topological data analysis (TDA), a method that identifies repeating geometrical motifs in space, was used to identify topological footprints underlying spatial arrangements of relevant cell types across histopathological progression.

Results We observed marked phenotypic heterogeneity between FA and non-FA sample groups, as well as across annotated tumor regions. FA tumors (HRD) showed reduced lymphocyte recruitment at early stages (LGD and HGD), followed by progressive myeloid enrichment at later stages (CIS and IC) in both epithelial and stromal compartments.

Neighborhood analysis identified that FA tumors had a progressive increase in stroma-restricted myeloid niches that correlated with TIM3-mediated T cell exhaustion, contrasting with canonical PD-1-associated exhaustion in non-FA samples (HRP). Finally, TDA found stable topological footprints scaffolding myeloid networks at immune exhaustion hotspots, suggesting an HRD-specific spatial organization of myeloid cells that contributes to impaired antitumoral responses.

Conclusion: We characterized the tumor microenvironment of HRD tumors using spatial proteomics and identified distinct topological arrangements associated with myeloid-mediated immunosuppression. This project provides a hypothesis-generating cellular and topological atlas of the TME of HRD tumors, focusing on patient stratification and alternative targeted therapies against exploring the inhibition of TIM3 ligands.
